title: Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic: Days of Fear

{{Quote|The arcs for 2007, not coincidentally, refer to a downward spiral of events spoken of in the movies by Yoda. And here, following the Mandalorian onslaught, things are beginning to go from bad to worse for the Republic. It's like 1940 in Europe, with one country falling after another.|John Jackson Miller|<ref name="SBC">{{WebCite|url=http://www.silverbulletcomicbooks.com/features/117807611743491.htm|text=John Jackson Miller: Understanding Star Wars|author=Matthew McLean|archivedate=20071031014859|nolive=1}}</ref>}}
'''''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic: Days of Fear''''' is the fourth story arc in the [[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic (comic series)|''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic'']] [[Star Wars comic books|comic book]] series and the first in the four-part ''Days/Nights'' cycle of story arcs. A three-part story arc, ''Days of Fear'' breaks up the core cast of the series, and also introduced the characters of [[Saul Karath]] and [[Carth Onasi]], who had previously appeared in the ''[[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic]]'' [[Star Wars video games|video game]].

==Plot summary==
The ''Last Resort'' leaves Zayne and Gryph on [[Ralltiir/Legends|Ralltiir]], where Gryph contracts [[Slyssk]], a [[Trandoshan/Legends|Trandoshan]] ship thief, in order to secure another ship. Unfortunately for them, the vessel that Slyssk steals, the ''[[Little Bivoli]]'', turns out to be a provisioning ship for the [[Republic Navy/Legends|Republic Navy]], and as such is noticed by the Republic fleet above Ralltiir.<ref name="KOTOR 13" /> Zayne, Gryph and Slyssk are forced to join the fleet, as it moves to the planet [[Serroco]], and pose as [[Fringer/Legends|fringers]], so as to avoid suspicion.<ref name="KOTOR 14" />

Meanwhile, having parted with Zayne and Gryph, the ''Last Resort'' heads for the Arkanian homeworld, [[Arkania/Legends|Arkania]], where Jarael hopes to find a cure for Camper's illness. They are engaged by an [[HK-24 series assassin droid]] sent after Camper, hidden inside a shipment crate.<ref name="KOTOR 13" /> After attempting to help Jarael (whom the invader does not care about) and Camper, [[T1-LB]] proves to be no match for the HK-24; however, [[Rohlan Dyre]], hiding aboard the ship, reveals himself and destroys the droid.<ref name="KOTOR 14" />

At [[Camp Three (Serroco)|Camp Three]] on Serroco, Zayne has a Force vision of the Mandalorians devastating the planet and tries to alert Gryph, but the latter dismisses his warnings, being too concerned about his own profit from the ''Little Bivoli'' fringer business.<ref name="KOTOR 14" /> Desperate to alert Saul Karath, now a [[Rear Admiral/Legends|Rear Admiral]], Zayne stows away on Lieutenant [[Carth Onasi]]'s ship when it heads for Karath's flagship, the [[Courageous (Inexpugnable-class)|''Courageous'']]; however, he is not believed and is detained on the ''Courageous'' as a suspected Mandalorian spy. Shortly thereafter, the Mandalorian fleet seen in Zayne's vision appears out of hyperspace. Strangely, though, the Mandalorian fleet does not engage the ''Courageous'', instead launching missiles around it toward the planet's surface.

While Carth manages to get seventeen of the [[Stereb cities]] evacuated underground, the [[Battle of Serroco]] results in a major disaster for the Republic.<ref name="KOTOR 15">[[Knights of the Old Republic 15|''Knights of the Old Republic'' 15]]</ref> The ''Little Bivoli'' is destroyed among most of the Republic fleet;<ref name="k16s">{{KOTORbackups|16}}</ref> Gryph's and Slyssk's respective fates were unknown after the battle.

==Development==
As ''Knights of the Old Republic'' became successful in early 2006, Miller began looking forward at 2007, and he developed an expansive storyline which would bring in multiple characters but could be broken down into smaller chunks so that the series' artists could alternate. This plan led to the development of four interlocking arcs: [[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic: Days of Fear|''Days of Fear'']], [[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic: Nights of Anger|''Nights of Anger'']], [[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic: Daze of Hate|''Daze of Hate'']], and [[Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic: Knights of Suffering|''Knights of Suffering'']]—the names of which stem from Jedi Master [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda's]] warning from [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''Star Wars'': Episode I ''The Phantom Menace'']]: "Fear leads to anger; anger leads to hate; hate leads to suffering."<ref name="Miller 13">{{Faraway|url=comics/swknights/swkotor13.html|text=''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic #13''}}</ref> The names of the arcs were first announced officially in the [[Drawn by the Force|Drawn By The Force]] department of [[Star Wars Insider 91|''Star Wars Insider'' 91]], which discussed the upcoming arcs.<ref name="Insider 91">{{InsiderCite|91|Jawa's Corner|Jawa's Corner: Comics}}</ref> While planning out the plots of the arcs, Miller was at one point forced to put every character's story path on a dry-erase board so that he could keep track of them all. Taking inspiration from [[Roy Thomas]]'s decision to split up the main characters of the Marvel ''Star Wars'' comics in [[Star Wars (1977) 7|''Star Wars'' (1977) 7]], Miller decided to break up the crew of ''The Last Resort'' in the opening pages of [[Knights of the Old Republic 13|''Knights of the Old Republic'' 13]].<ref name="Miller 13" /> The four arc titles came to be referred to collectively as ''Days/Knights'' first unofficially by Miller himself<ref name="Insider 91 discussion">{{DHBoards|subdomain=www|url=boards/viewtopic.php?t=10614|thread=KOTOR story arcs from Insider 91|date=2006-12-10}}</ref> and later officially, as part of the publisher's summary for [[Knights of the Old Republic 23|issue #23]].<ref name="DH 23">{{DarkHorse|url=Comics/14-776/Star-Wars-Knights-of-the-Old-Republic-23--Knights-of-Suffering-part-2|text=Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic #23—Knights of Suffering part 2}}</ref> Miller later confirmed that the look that Carrick gives the "camera" in a scene in Weaver's ''Days of Fear, Part 1'' was inspired by the look that [[Wikipedia:Jim Halpert|Jim]], a character from the US version of the television show [[Wikipedia:The Office (US TV series)|''The Office'']], often gives the camera.<ref name="Jim">{{DHBoards|subdomain=www|url=boards/viewtopic.php?t=10868&postdays=0&postorder=asc&start=60|thread=KotOR #13 -- Consolidate your life debts!|date=2007-02-05|time=10:20 am|user=[[John Jackson Miller]]}}</ref>

Also in issue #13, the creative team introduced Zayne Carrick's phrikite vambraces, the character's first unique piece of equipment and an element that had been a part of Brian Ching's design for the character since his inception. Dustin Weaver handled artwork duties for issues #13 and [[Knights of the Old Republic 15|#15]], while Brian Ching drew [[Knights of the Old Republic 14|issue #14]], the second part of ''Days of Fear''. ''Days of Fear'' also saw the return of the backup news organs, with ''[[The Admiral's List: Jimas Veltraa Memorial Edition]]'', ''[[The Adjudicator Special Report: The Outer Rim]]'', and ''[[The Taris Holofeed: Siege Edition]]'' accompanying the three issues of the arc respectively. The character of Slyssk was partially inspired by Miller's childhood action figure of the Trandoshan bounty hunter [[Bossk/Legends|Bossk]], which had wider eyes than in [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''The Empire Strikes Back'']], and the name of the ''Little Bivoli'' —a ship designed by Dustin Weaver—is a reference to a foodstuff in the MMORPG [[Star Wars Galaxies|''Star Wars Galaxies: An Empire Divided'']]. The ''Reliance''{{'s}} name comes from the same source as that of the ''Courageous'': a ship in the British Navy of the Napoleonic era of the same class as the ''Courageaux''.<ref name="Miller 13" /> Miller's grandfather served aboard a large vessel known as a [[Wikipedia:Landing Ship, Tank|Landing Ship-Tank]] in [[Wikipedia:World War II|World War II]], and the LSTs—which were used to deploy smaller vehicles and machinery onto battlefields—inspired the scenes on Serroco's surface, particularly those that dealt with the logistics of the Republic Navy. The ''Deadweight'' was also inspired by the LSTs; the ''[[Knights of the Old Republic Campaign Guide]]'' identified the ship's original designation as CBX-560, a reference to [[Wikipedia:USS LST-560|USS ''LST-560'']], the ship that Miller's grandfather served on.<ref name="Countdown 6">{{Faraway|url=blog/2010/02/14/kotor-countdown-6-large-slow-targets|text=KOTOR Countdown #6: Large, Slow Targets take to space}}</ref>

[[File:Carthonasipossible.jpg|thumb|right|150px|The Carth Onasi/John Jackson Miller look-alike]]
''Days of Fear, Part 2'' introduces the character of Carth Onasi, a companion character in the original ''Knights of the Old Republic'' game, and the creative team worked hard to ensure that he was consistent with his game portrayal and yet also present surprises. Looking back at the issue, Miller acknowledged the fact that he unknowingly staged the dialogue similar to that of the game, in that Carrick—like the player character—receives long expositions from Onasi in response to single-line observations and questions. Miller and Ching plotted out a vision of Serroco's destruction that was relatively similar to how the actual event would appear, and in the production notes for the issue, Miller acknowledged the questions of fans as to whether a bearded crew member on the ''Courageous'' from issue #8 was Onasi. Miller had intended Onasi to be present at Vanquo on the ''Courageous'', but he did not specifically direct Dustin Weaver to draw the character, and Miller himself assumed that Weaver had drawn Onasi—only to later learn from Weaver that the crewmember was actually a rendition of Miller himself.<ref name="Miller 14">{{Faraway|url=comics/swknights/swkotor14.html|text=''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic #14''}}</ref> Zayne Carrick's reaction to the death of the Sterebs in ''Days of Fear, Part 3'' was inspired Miller's personal reaction to the [[Wikipedia:September 11 attacks|events]] of [[September 11]], [[2001]].<ref name="Miller 15">{{Faraway|url=comics/swknights/swkotor15.html|text=''Star Wars: Knights of the Old Republic #15''}}</ref> Ching and Atiyeh drew the cover artwork for ''Days of Fear'',<ref name="KOTOR 13" /><ref name="KOTOR 14" /><ref name="KOTOR 15" /> and the cover for ''Part 3'' was directly suggested by Miller: he noted to Brian Ching how he liked one of Frank Miller's covers for [[Wikipedia:List of Daredevil titles|''Daredevil'']] that featured [[Wikipedia:Daredevil (Marvel Comics)|Daredevil]] cowering on a white background. Ching took one of Carrick's postures in the previous issue and added Mandalore's mask and the flames. An earlier promotional image featured a redder mask, though the mask was ultimately changed to a brighter gold by the time of the release.<ref name="Miller 15" /> Issue #15 was delayed for two weeks from its original scheduled release date of [[March 28]], and was instead published on [[April 11]].<ref name="KOTOR 15" />
